story:
Shilvana was six when the masters first severed her hand.

She had been waiting for this day. After all, that was their purpose: food for the Cromera. If her body parts meant safety for the elves of Farizia, then this was nothing but an honourable sacrifice.

The Cromera protected all, weathering the storms that forever threatened the city. In return, the masters would sustain Her with the younglings. Before puberty, Shilvana’s kind had the gift to regenerate. Her hand would grow back in a matter of days.

She just did not know it would hurt so bad.

She was fortunate. The Cromera only wanted her hand. Her best friend Erikh had almost died from bleeding when he lost both his arms at previous feeding. 

Erikh imagined the Cromera as a shining beauty with star-bright eyes and a halo of hair. Shilvana teased him, but she liked his hope. All the younglings agreed on one thing: Her voice. Thin as flute wind yet strong enough to crack a heart, rising into a choir that sounded like thousands. When the Cromera sang, trees seemed to dance. Night after night Shilvana and Erikh sat by the window, listening, caught between comfort and a lump in the throat. She would understand the lump later. By then it would be too late to cry.

They celebrated Marizka’s release on her fifteenth birthday. Shy, bookish Marizka—now a lady—was the oldest among them, all delicate curves and hidden courage. Erikh loved her. The masters threw a feast: pies, chocolate rolls, taro crispies. They even brought out Bogimashes, fifteen slithery bodies hung like ribbons in a corner, one for each cycle Marizka had served in the Dorm—what the masters cheerfully called the Cromera’s grocery barn. The Bogimashes pulsed and flashed, beating out music that set the glass floor alive with color. The younglings danced. The masters smiled too, but their smiles lingered strangely, like a shadow behind light.

On the last night before Erikh’s puberty, the two sat together. Shilvana noted that the Cromera had tasted every part of her but her head. Erikh said he had given even his ears and tongue. They never spoke of the pain—sacrilege, the masters said—but Shilvana had questions. Questions earned lashes and a night in chains, and Erikh’s hurt silence afterward. 

The Cromera sang. Erikh turned his head to the walls, tears welled up in his eyes. One night. One night before he would leave her. This could be the last chance he had to ever see the Cromera. Shilvana knew he wanted to. He—they—had the right to know what it was that had placed them in this predicament, honourably or not. Shilvana took his hand. They ran.

They slipped past kitchens and library, through the masters’ floor, and out the garden window. Two masters guarded the gate. A quick plan—two stones like Puka balls—and a scramble. One hit, a shove, a shoe, and the guards were down. They crawled to the wall and found a fist-sized gap. The Cromera’s music stopped.

Light spilled through. Inside were twenty women, all chained to narrow beds beneath an awning marked Harvesting ground 52. Their faces were rimmed with tears. All were pregnant. Elf or youngling, Shilvana could not tell. At the far corner sat Marizka, gagged, crying, a master stroking her hair as if soothing her. On a table nearby lay a severed hand on a plate. The master ate from it and pressed pieces toward Marizka’s mouth.

The truth cut like a blade. There were no elves, not as they had been told. No Farizia waiting with padded walls and safety. The “masters” were grown males of their own kind, no longer able to regenerate and hiding behind a story. The Cromera was not a goddess, but the system that raised girls to breed and boys to rule, a machine of songs and lies that turned children into food and mothers into fields.

She felt Erikh drop to the ground, curling into a ball and sobbing uncontrollably next to her.

Her best friend. Her future enemy.

Shilvana retched.
